one_line_takeaway: With 1,276 people scoring 51.2% near a coin toss at spotting AI-generated media in a simulated feed, and prior deepfake awareness providing no benefit, the paper argues human perceptual detection is no longer a viable defense against weaponized synthetic media and that technical/provenance countermeasures are now essential.
key_finding: Overall detection was 51.2%, statistically near the 50% chance floor, with strikingly little spread across modalities (image 49.4% worst, audio 53.7%, audiovisual 54.5% best). Detection was strongly asymmetric by authenticity: people correctly identified authentic stimuli 64.6% of the time but synthetic stimuli only 38.8% a real-world bias toward believing content is genuine, which is the dangerous direction. Synthetic human-face images (46.6%) were harder to flag than animals (51.7%), food (49.9%), or landscapes (54.7%). Adding a second modality helped: the same 30 videos were detected at 55.9% with audio vs. 50.7% video-only. Language familiarity helped modestly (known 54.5% vs. foreign 51.3%). Heterogeneous audiovisual fakes (synthetic video + authentic audio, 43.4%) were harder to catch than fully-synthetic ones (49.0%). Older participants performed significantly worse (especially on audio/audiovisual). Notably, self-reported prior deepfake knowledgeability had NO significant effect (unfamiliar 51.1%, semi 51.0%, highly familiar 51.9%; ANOVA p=0.25) suggesting awareness alone does not confer perceptual protection.
core_contribution: A large pre-registered perceptual study (1,276 participants, 124,187 retained observations) measuring how well ordinary people can distinguish authentic from AI-generated media across four modalities (images, audio-only, video-only, and audiovisual) under conditions deliberately engineered to emulate passive social-media newsfeed browsing (self-paced exposure, no feedback, unknown synthetic-to-authentic ratio, vertical scrolling). The paper's own headline result is that unaided human perceptual detection is essentially at chance (mean 51.2%), and it quantifies how specific stimulus characteristics and observer demographics push detection above or below that floor. The authors position this as evidence that human perceptual detection can no longer serve as a defense against weaponized synthetic media and that alternative countermeasures (machine detection, watermarking, provenance, hashing, media-literacy training) are required. They also claim several first-of-kind contributions: including synthetic images of animals, testing the role of spoken-language familiarity in visual/audiovisual detection, comparing heterogeneous vs. fully-synthetic audiovisual stimuli, and examining prior self-reported deepfake knowledgeability as a factor.
threat_model: Adversaries weaponize publicly-available generative AI (commercial products and open-source software, pre-2024, spanning GANs, diffusion models, and generative autoencoders) to produce convincing synthetic images/audio/video for disinformation, financial fraud, non-consensual intimate imagery (including of minors), espionage, and scams. The 'defense' under evaluation is the targeted individual's own perceptual ability to recognize synthetic content when encountered in an online feed. The paper's finding is that this defense fails: at ~chance detection, humans cannot be relied upon to self-protect. This is framed as a social-engineering / presentation-attack surface (the paper's CCS concepts explicitly list social engineering attacks and intrusion/anomaly detection). Note this is a human-perception study, not an ML attack/defense paper; there is no algorithmic adversary being optimized against.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) generative AI has advanced since the pre-2024 stimuli were sourced, so present-day detection may be even worse than reported; (2) the newsfeed simulation deliberately stripped captions, engagement metrics, comments, and any ability to cross-reference/fact-check, so results are ecologically valid only for context-limited, non-critical passive consumption not for engaged users who verify sources; (3) language fluency and prior-knowledge levels were self-reported and never tested, so mis-estimation is possible. Reviewer-added: the logistic model explains little variance (Nagelkerke R2 = 0.095; age model R2 = 0.04), so the identified factors, while significant given the huge N, account for only a small fraction of detection variability many effect sizes are small (Cohen's d 0.04-0.21). The near-chance mean partly reflects the study's hard design choices (no feedback, unknown base rate, self-paced browsing); it is a claim about detection under those specific conditions, not an absolute ceiling on human ability. Datasets were not public at publication (available on request). Model provenance for many audio/video stimuli was unknown, and stimulus quality was curated to be 'representative' by the authors rather than benchmarked.

datasets_used: StyleGAN / Alias-Free GAN datasets - synthetic human-face images
Open-source/public GAN and latent-diffusion (LD) datasets - synthetic non-face images
Custom stimulus corpus: 194 total stimuli (96 images: 48 faces, 26 animals, 12 landscapes, 10 food; 30 audio clips across 13 languages; 38 audiovisual + 30 video-only clips)
124,187 retained classification observations from 1,276 Prolific participants (pre-registration OSF osf.io/fnhr3)
quantitative_results: By modality: image 49.4% (worst), video-only 50.7%, audio-only 53.7%, audiovisual 54.5% (best)
Authenticity asymmetry: authentic stimuli 64.6% vs. synthetic stimuli 38.8%
Heterogeneous audiovisual (synthetic video + authentic audio) 43.4% vs. fully-synthetic audiovisual 49.0% (t(9861)=6.3, p<0.001, Cohen's d=0.11)
Image subject matter: human faces 46.6% vs. animals 51.7%, food 49.9%, landscapes 54.7% (t(12201)=-13.83, p<0.001, d=0.21)
Same 30 videos: audiovisual 55.9% vs. video-only 50.7% (t(37517)=10.15, p<0.001, d=0.11); multimodal vs single-modality overall d=0.4
Language familiarity: known 54.5% vs. foreign 51.3% overall; audio known 55.3% vs foreign 52.3%; audiovisual known 56.5% vs foreign 52.0%; video known 52.0% vs foreign 49.5%
Age: significant negative predictor (beta=-56.64, R2=0.04, F(1,1274)=55.63, p<0.001); largest decline for audio and audiovisual
Prior knowledgeability: no significant effect - unfamiliar 51.1%, semi-familiar 51.0%, highly familiar 51.9% (one-way ANOVA F(2,124185)=1.4, p=0.25, eta-squared<0.01)
Primary logistic regression: Nagelkerke R2=0.095, likelihood-ratio chi-square(6)=9142.5, p<0.001; Bonferroni-corrected alpha=0.0031
baselines_compared: Random-chance performance (50%) - the primary reference point; observed mean 51.2%

Within-study condition contrasts (authentic vs synthetic; face vs non-face; single- vs multi-modal; known vs foreign language; heterogeneous vs fully-synthetic audiovisual; younger vs older; familiarity tiers)

Qualitative comparison to prior human-detection literature (e.g. Groh et al. 2023 audio-improves-video finding; Muller et al. 2022 native-speaker advantage; Nightingale & Farid 2022 / Tucciarelli 2022 near-chance faces; Josephs et al. 2023 environmental-factor degradation) - no computational/ML detector baseline is run in this paper
